Name: RateDistortionOptimalPruning
Title: Optimal Pruning via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: Pruning neural networks can be optimized using rate-distortion theory to achieve the best trade-off between model size and task performance.
Related Work: Recent work has explored the use of rate-distortion theory for model compression and pruning, such as Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019). However, these studies often focus on theoretical bounds or specific pruning techniques. Our proposal aims to derive a principled, information-theoretic method for pruning that directly optimizes the rate-distortion trade-off, ensuring that the model is compressed as much as possible while preserving task performance.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to model pruning using rate-distortion theory. By framing pruning as a lossy source coding problem, we aim to find the optimal trade-off between model size (rate) and task performance (distortion). Our method involves deriving a rate-distortion function that quantifies the minimum achievable distortion for a given rate of compression. We then use this function to guide the pruning process, ensuring that the model is compressed as much as possible while maintaining or even improving task performance. We validate our approach through experiments on standard benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing pruning techniques.
Experiments: Derive the rate-distortion function for a neural network, using mutual information as the distortion measure.
Develop an algorithm to iteratively prune the network while approximating the rate-distortion function.
Evaluate the pruned models on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to measure the trade-off between model size and task performance.
Compare the performance of our method against state-of-the-art pruning techniques, both in terms of compression ratio and task accuracy.
Analyze the impact of different distortion metrics (e.g., mutual information, cross-entropy) on the pruning process and the final model performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The derivation of the rate-distortion function may be complex and require significant computational resources.
The choice of distortion metric can significantly affect the results, and finding the right metric for different tasks may be challenging.
The iterative pruning process may not always converge to the optimal solution, and hyperparameter tuning may be necessary.
The method may not generalize well to all types of neural network architectures and datasets.
